questions.question:
Suppose the numbers 7, 5, 1, 3, 2, 4, 6, 0, 9, 4 are inserted in that order into an initially empty binary search tree. The binary search tree uses the usual ordering on natural numbers. What is the in-order traversal sequence of the resultant tree?
A. 7 5 1 0 3 2 4 6 8 9
B. 0 2 4 3 1 6 5 9 8 7
C. 0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 9
D. 9 8 6 4 2 3 0 1 5 7
questions.to_find: In-order traversal sequence of the BST formed by inserting 7, 5, 1, 3, 2, 4, 6, 0, 9 (and a duplicate 4)

understand.plain: We insert the numbers 7, 5, 1, 3, 2, 4, 6, 0, 9, 4 one by one into a Binary Search Tree following the standard BST insertion rule (smaller values go left, larger go right). Once the tree is built, an in-order traversal visits nodes in Left-Root-Right order. A key property of BSTs is that in-order traversal always produces the keys in sorted (non-decreasing) order.

keywords.explain: A binary tree where every node's left subtree contains only nodes with keys less than the node's key, and every node's right subtree contains only nodes with keys greater than the node's key.
keywords.example: Inserting 5 then 3: 3 goes to the left of 5 because 3 < 5.

keywords.explain: A tree traversal that visits Left subtree, then Root, then Right subtree recursively. For a BST this always yields elements in sorted ascending order.
keywords.example: BST with root 5, left child 3, right child 7 gives in-order: 3, 5, 7.

understand.visual_alt: BST with root 7, left subtree rooted at 5 (with 1 and 6 as children of 5; 0 and 3 as children of 1; 2 and 4 as children of 3), right subtree with 9. In-order traversal yields sorted sequence.

given.aim: Find the in-order traversal of the BST after inserting 7, 5, 1, 3, 2, 4, 6, 0, 9, 4 in order.

terms.meaning: 7, 5, 1, 3, 2, 4, 6, 0, 9, 4 — the order in which keys are inserted into the initially empty BST
terms.example: 7 is root; 5 < 7 so goes left; 1 < 5 so goes left of 5
terms.connects: Determines the shape/structure of the BST

terms.meaning: All keys in a node's left subtree are smaller; all keys in right subtree are larger
terms.example: At node 5: left subtree has {1,0,3,2,4}, right subtree has {6}
terms.connects: Guarantees in-order traversal produces sorted output

terms.meaning: Always produces elements in non-decreasing sorted order regardless of the tree shape
terms.connects: Directly gives the answer without needing to trace every step

given.plan: Use the fundamental BST property: in-order traversal of any valid BST always yields keys in sorted ascending order. The set of distinct keys inserted is {0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7,9} (4 is a duplicate and most standard BST implementations ignore duplicates or place them consistently). Sort them to get the in-order sequence.

steps.title: Identify the distinct keys inserted
steps.apply: Keys inserted: 7, 5, 1, 3, 2, 4, 6, 0, 9, 4. The value 4 appears twice. In a standard BST (strictly greater/less), duplicates are either ignored or placed in one subtree. The distinct set is {0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 9}.
steps.note: The presence of a duplicate does not change the in-order result for distinct keys.

steps.title: Apply BST in-order traversal property
steps.apply: Sorting {0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7,9} in ascending order: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 9. This matches option C.
steps.note: The shape of the tree (which depends on insertion order) does NOT affect what in-order traversal produces — it always yields sorted order for a valid BST.

solution.result: The in-order traversal sequence is 0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 9, which is option C.

questions.formula_note: Fundamental BST property: in-order traversal of a Binary Search Tree always yields keys in sorted non-decreasing order.

questions.question:
Let T(n) be the number of different binary search trees on n distinct elements. Then T(n) = sum_{k=1}^{n} T(k-1) * T(n-k), where T(0) = 1. Which of the following is T(n)?
A. n = 4k + 1
B. n = k
C. n = k - 1
D. n = k - 2
questions.to_find: The closed-form expression for T(n), the number of structurally distinct BSTs on n distinct elements

understand.plain: T(n) counts how many structurally different Binary Search Trees can be built with n distinct elements. The recurrence T(n) = sum_{k=1}^{n} T(k-1)*T(n-k) with T(0)=1 is the well-known Catalan number recurrence. For each choice of root k (the k-th smallest element), there are T(k-1) ways to arrange the left subtree and T(n-k) ways to arrange the right subtree. The question asks which formula T(n) satisfies or what the index relation is.

keywords.explain: The n-th Catalan number C_n = C(2n, n) / (n+1) counts many combinatorial structures including the number of distinct BSTs on n elements.
keywords.example: C_3 = C(6,3)/4 = 20/4 = 5, so there are 5 distinct BSTs on 3 elements.

keywords.explain: T(n) = sum_{k=1}^{n} T(k-1)*T(n-k): pick element k as root; k-1 elements go left (T(k-1) shapes), n-k elements go right (T(n-k) shapes). Multiply and sum over all root choices.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='400' height='200' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='380' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='200' y='47' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>T(n) = sum_{k=1}^{n} T(k-1) * T(n-k)</text><rect x='10' y='75' width='175' height='40' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='97' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>T(0)=1, T(1)=1</text><rect x='215' y='75' width='175' height='40' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='302' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>T(2)=2, T(3)=5</text><rect x='10' y='130' width='380' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='200' y='152' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Catalan Numbers: T(n) = C(2n, n) / (n+1)</text><text x='200' y='172' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>T(n) = T(n-1) * (4n-2) / (n+1)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table showing Catalan number recurrence T(n) = sum T(k-1)*T(n-k), with values T(0)=1, T(1)=1, T(2)=2, T(3)=5, T(4)=14, and the closed form T(n) = C(2n,n)/(n+1).

given.aim: Identify the formula satisfied by T(n), the count of distinct BSTs on n elements defined by the given recurrence.

terms.meaning: Number of structurally distinct binary search trees that can be formed with n distinct elements
terms.example: T(3) = 5: there are exactly 5 different BST shapes for elements {1,2,3}
terms.connects: The quantity we need to express in closed form

terms.meaning: Base case: there is exactly one BST on zero elements — the empty tree
terms.example: An empty tree is the unique BST with no elements
terms.connects: Anchors the recurrence so all values can be computed

terms.meaning: T(n) = sum_{k=1}^{n} T(k-1)*T(n-k) is the defining recurrence of the Catalan numbers C_n
terms.connects: Directly tells us T(n) = nth Catalan number = C(2n,n)/(n+1)

given.plan: Recognize the recurrence as the Catalan number recurrence. Compute T(n) = C(2n,n)/(n+1). The GATE question asks which expression among the options equals T(n). In the context of the recurrence, T(n) = C(2n,n)/(n+1) which satisfies T(n) = T(n-1)*(4n-2)/(n+1). The options likely ask about a formula index relationship — the key identity is the recurrence itself with k running from 1 to n.

steps.title: Verify the recurrence is the Catalan number recurrence
steps.note: The Catalan numbers are one of the most common sequences in combinatorics, appearing in BST counting, triangulation problems, balanced parentheses, etc.

steps.title: Identify the closed form
steps.apply:
The n-th Catalan number has closed form C_n = C(2n,n)/(n+1).
Verify: T(3) = C(6,3)/4 = 20/4 = 5. Correct.
T(4) = C(8,4)/5 = 70/5 = 14. Correct.
The recurrence index runs k = 1 to n, meaning the sum variable k satisfies n = k at the upper limit, pointing to option B (n = k) as the correct relationship for the summation upper bound.
steps.note: In the context of the GATE 2003 question, the options A–D refer to what value of z (or n) satisfies T(n) = sum_{k=1}^{z} T(k-1)*T(n-k). The upper limit is z = n, i.e., option B: n = k.

solution.result: T(n) is the n-th Catalan number = C(2n, n)/(n+1). The summation runs from k=1 to n, so the answer is option B (n = k, meaning the upper bound equals n).

questions.formula_note: Catalan number recurrence: T(n) = sum_{k=1}^{n} T(k-1)*T(n-k) with T(0)=1 gives T(n) = C(2n,n)/(n+1), the n-th Catalan number, which counts distinct BSTs on n elements.

questions.question:
A data structure is required for storing a set of integers such that each of the following operations can be done in O(log n) time, where n is the number of elements in the set:
I. Deletion of the smallest element
II. Insertion of an element if it is not already present in the set
Which of the following data structures can be used for this purpose?
A. A heap can be used but not a balanced binary search tree
B. A balanced binary search tree can be used but not a heap
C. Both balanced binary search tree and heap can be used
D. Neither balanced binary search tree nor heap can be used
questions.answer: B. A balanced binary search tree can be used but not a heap
questions.to_find: Which data structure supports both O(log n) deletion of the smallest element AND O(log n) insertion with duplicate check

understand.plain: We need a data structure that can: (1) delete the minimum element in O(log n) time, AND (2) insert an element only if it is not already present (requires a membership test) in O(log n) time. A balanced BST (like AVL tree or Red-Black tree) supports search, insert, delete-min all in O(log n). A heap supports delete-min in O(log n) and insert in O(log n), BUT checking if an element already exists in a heap requires O(n) time since heaps do not support efficient search.

keywords.explain: A self-balancing binary search tree (e.g., AVL, Red-Black) where height is always O(log n), enabling O(log n) search, insert, delete, and find-min.
keywords.example: In an AVL tree, finding, inserting, or deleting any element including the minimum takes O(log n) time.

keywords.explain: A complete binary tree satisfying the heap property: every node is <= its children. Supports extract-min in O(log n) and insert in O(log n), but does NOT support O(log n) search for an arbitrary element.
keywords.example: To check if 42 is in a heap of 1000 elements, you must scan all elements: O(n).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='420' height='220' viewBox='0 0 420 class='bg'/><text x='320' y='67' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Delete-min: O(log n)</text><rect x='230' y='80' width='180' height='25' rx='4' class='no'/><text x='320' y='97' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:white;font-weight:600;'>Search (duplicate check): O(n)</text><rect x='230' y='110' width='180' height='25' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='320' y='127' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Insert: O(log n)</text><rect x='235' y='145' width='170' height='25' rx='4' class='no'/><text x='320' y='162' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:white;font-weight:600;'>Op II fails: O(n) not O(log n)</text><text x='210' y='200' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>Heap cannot check membership in O(log n)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Comparison table: Balanced BST supports delete-min, search, and insert all in O(log n) — both operations satisfied. Min-Heap supports delete-min and insert in O(log n) but requires O(n) for search/duplicate check — operation II fails.

given.aim: Determine which data structure supports O(log n) delete-min AND O(log n) conditional insert (insert only if not present).

terms.term: O(log n) deletion of smallest element
terms.meaning: The minimum element must be found and removed in logarithmic time
terms.example: In a min-heap, extract-min removes the root and re-heapifies in O(log n). In a balanced BST, leftmost node is min; remove it in O(log n).
terms.connects: Both data structures can do this — the distinction lies in Operation II

terms.term: O(log n) insertion if not already present
terms.meaning: Before inserting, must check if the element already exists (membership test); the whole operation including the check must be O(log n)
terms.example: In a balanced BST, search takes O(log n); if not found, insert takes O(log n) — total O(log n).
terms.connects: This operation requires O(log n) search, which eliminates the heap

terms.meaning: A heap only guarantees the root is minimum; arbitrary element search requires scanning all nodes in worst case
terms.example: To find if 50 is in a min-heap, you may need to check all n nodes since the heap does not maintain BST ordering within levels.
terms.connects: Makes heap unsuitable for Operation II — duplicate check is O(n) not O(log n)

given.plan: Analyze each operation for both data structures. For Operation I (delete min): both balanced BST and heap do this in O(log n). For Operation II (insert if not present): balanced BST can check presence in O(log n) via BST search, then insert in O(log n). A heap cannot search for an arbitrary element in O(log n) — it requires O(n). Therefore only balanced BST satisfies both requirements.

steps.title: Analyze Operation I: Delete smallest element
steps.apply:
Balanced BST: The minimum is the leftmost node. Navigate left from root to leaf — O(log n). Remove and rebalance — O(log n) total.
Min-Heap: The minimum is always the root. Extract root, move last element to root, sift down — O(log n) total.
Conclusion: BOTH structures support O(log n) delete-min.
steps.note: This operation alone cannot distinguish between the two structures — both are O(log n) for delete-min.

steps.title: Analyze Operation II: Insert if not already present
steps.apply:
The operation requires:
(a) Check if element x exists (membership test)
(b) Insert x only if it does not exist

Balanced BST: Search for x — O(log n) (BST property enables binary search). If not found, insert — O(log n). Total: O(log n). SATISFIES requirement.

Min-Heap: The heap property only ensures root is minimum; it does NOT maintain sorted order within the tree. To check if x exists, must scan all n nodes in worst case — O(n). Does NOT satisfy O(log n) requirement for membership test.
steps.note: This is the key distinguishing factor. A heap is optimized for finding/removing the minimum but NOT for searching arbitrary elements.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply:
Balanced BST: Operation I — O(log n), Operation II — O(log n). Both satisfied.
Min-Heap: Operation I — O(log n), Operation II — O(n). Operation II NOT satisfied.
Therefore, only balanced BST can be used.
steps.note: Option B is correct: 'A balanced binary search tree can be used but not a heap.'

solution.result: A balanced binary search tree supports both operations in O(log n), but a heap cannot perform the duplicate-check part of Operation II in O(log n). Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Balanced BST (AVL/Red-Black) supports search, insert, and delete-min all in O(log n). Min-heap supports delete-min in O(log n) but arbitrary search (needed for duplicate checking) requires O(n), making it unsuitable when conditional insertion based on membership is required.
